title: The Phantom Warning
text: I was drifting through town, lost in thought,when i stepped into the street without looking. A piercing shriek sliced through the air-an old woman, her voice croking the dead leaves, screamed for me to stop.  I froze and turned just in time to see a bus thunder past, a blast air claeing at the face.  My heart pounded as I looked accross to thank her, but the street was empty. No trace of her remained.  That's when the chill set in-she looked exactly like my grandmother who we buried seven years ago. I stil remember the floral scarf she wore... the same one we buried her in

title: The Pianist's Lament
text: We wandered into the skeleton of an abandoned theather, its air thick wit forgetten breath. As we crept through its crumbling halss, a paino melody began to drift-low, haunting almost mournful.  It drew us to the stage, where a dust-veiled paino sat, keys untounched, strings long dead. Then he appeared: a tall, gaunt figure in tattered evening wear, standing in the balcony like a relic of grief. He stared down at us, his eyes hollow with memory, before dissolving into nothingness.  Later, i learned of the fire-the painist who perished mid-performance, still searching for his final ovation.

title: The widow in the Rocking Chair
text: The hostel was ancient, its walls whispering with age, At 2AM, I woke to the slow cracking of footsteps accross my room, soft and deliberate.  I called out, but no answer caame, i grapple with thwe light switch. As my eyes adjusted to the pale, sickly glow that spilled accross the room, I noticed the rocking chair in the corner, moving to and fro as if soeone has just stand from it. I watched it until it came to a dead, silent stop. In the gray light of dawn, i told the hostel owner. she gave me a knowing glance. 'That chair belonged to Edith. She lost her husband at sea and never left this room agian. You're not the first to feel her presence.'

title: A Reflection Stayed
text:
Outside, the shutters of all the shops were closed. Everything looked as if it were bathed in moonlight. Pink petals drifted here and there, and ivy crept along the building walls. A man wandered aimlessly around; it didn't look like he was searching for anyone.

​He spoke to himself. "This was the only place where I could truly live out my childhood; in the city, I was mostly kept locked away. I used to come here during my vacations when I was a child," the man said. Just then, a voice echoes from somewhere: "You never really stopped coming here." He either didn't hear it, or perhaps didn't want to.

​He walked inside a tunnel, where moss clung to the walls like a painting, and even inside, rays of sunlight filtered through. Water pooled on the ground, with leaves floating on top. He walked along the tracks, which had rusted over and were bound by fountain grass. Walking along, he arrived at an empty station. An unfamiliar fragrance of metal and earth hung in the air, which he deeply inhaled.

​Just then, his eyes fell upon a black-and-white cat. "Is that you?" he said, stepping toward it, but the cat ran away. ​“She’s gone... Her name is Rani. I've been seeing her since childhood; my grandmother raised her,” he said. ​Just then, in the water pooled at his feet, his own reflection screams: "That isn't her at all! It's impossible for a cat to live for this many years!"

​Looking up at those tangled wires, his gaze shifted to the dark, thundering clouds. And the first drop of rain fell onto his face. "Good thing I haven't bathed yet. Now I can bathe in the rain just like I used to." Just then, through those falling raindrops, his reflection says: "You didn't always get to bathe in the rain; you used to fall sick."

​On his way back, he stopped by a park. "I always used to play here with my friends. How strange... I didn't make as many good memories in the city during the entire year as I did in this town." His gaze fell upon an old, torn teddy bear that lay in the mud, getting drenched. He picked it up, set it on top of the slide, and placed a broken umbrella over it. "I wish you had friends too, and you could play with them just like I used to play in my childhood," he said to the teddy bear. He began to walk away, but his own voice echoes back from the teddy bear as his reflection whispers: "You wished someone had played with it. But this was your teddy bear. They tore it apart... and they beat you for trying to take it back."

​Everything began to gleam under the downpour. "People have many reasons for leaving a place, but the biggest reason is their growing up. People do grow up here, but as soon as they grow up, they leave this place. And those who are already grown up either leave this town, or they leave this world..." the man said to himself as he walked along. This time, his reflection—which is forming in the empty buses, the washed-out roads, and the rain-slicked poles—says nothing. It only listens to his words intently.

​Walking along, he stopped near a house and said, "This is the house where that girl used to live. We never spoke a word out loud; all our conversations happened through our eyes. I didn't arrive here on time, and she left this place too. Forgive me, our story remained incomplete," he said, looking toward that window. "Time didn't wait for either of you," his reflection whispers.

​Night fell, and the crescent moon woke up, filling the wet sky with its light. The wet ground began to reflect the glow from the street lamps, as if a golden path had been painted everywhere. The man peeked out at all of this from his bedroom window. But outside, sitting, his reflection watched him back.

​Looking out from his window, the man said, "Yes, this place is no longer the same. I can come here, but I cannot bring back that lively spirit. A place is defined by its people. If my friends and cousins were still here, we would have been up on the roof since evening. In fact, everyone in the town used to gather on their roofs by evening. What I am feeling right now, I cannot put into words. They're gone now. Even so, when I remember them, I can't tell whether I'm smiling because they happened... or grieving because they'll never happen again."

​Sitting outside next to that black-and-white cat, his reflection says to the cat, "Even if the good memories weigh more, the bad ones deserve to be remembered too. When will you understand this? Do you think I will ever be able to set him free?"

​The cat blinked slowly. For the first time, the reflection smiles.
